# Antiphon

AI moderator for multi-party video meetings. Brennan-Lee-Mulligan-DM energy, not librarian energy. Active conductor, executive floor control, public reason-tagged bids, private speaker HUD.

Status: **design sketch** (see [docs/antiphon.md](docs/antiphon.md)) + early spikes.

## Layout

```
cmd/
  antiphon-relay/   v1 audio-relay wedge (sits in front of Zoom, mutes/ducks on command)
  antiphon-mod/     moderator/allocator daemon
internal/
  allocator/        floor-allocation policy
  bid/              continuous bid intensity + reason tags
  relay/            audio relay primitives (gain, control socket)
  policy/           per-meeting modes (brainstorm/decide/inform/socialize)
  hud/              private speaker HUD
docs/
  antiphon.md       the design sketch — read this first
  decisions/        ADRs
  literature/       paper notes
  open-questions/   the 7 open questions, one per file
prototypes/
  audio-relay-spike/    proof: PCM through a Go gain controller via UDS
  bid-heatmap-ui/       proof: continuous bid + reason-tagged UI feel
scripts/                audio plumbing helpers (pactl/pw-cli wiring)
```

## Language choices

- **Go** for v1 audio relay, allocator, policy, backend services. No Zoom SDK touches this layer.
- **TypeScript/React** for any UI prototype + future Zoom Apps SDK panel (JS forced by Zoom).
- **C++ or Recall.ai** if we ever do option (a) bot-as-host — Go has no Zoom bot bindings.

See [docs/decisions/0001-language-choice.md](docs/decisions/0001-language-choice.md).

## v1 wedge

Audio relay (deployment shape **c** in the design doc): keep the user on Zoom, route their mic through a Go process that can ([0,1]-gain) mute/duck/pass on command from the allocator. No Zoom SDK required. See `prototypes/audio-relay-spike/`.

# ADR 0001 — Language choice against Zoom's SDK landscape

**Date**: 2026-05-20
**Status**: accepted

## Question

User asked: "if this needs to be a zoom sdk app then check whether go makes sense."

## Zoom SDK reality (as of 2026-05)

| SDK / surface | Language |
|---|---|
| **Zoom Apps SDK** — in-meeting side panel (iframe in Zoom client) | JS/TS only |
| **Zoom Meeting SDK** — embed Zoom client in your app | C++, .NET, iOS, Android, macOS, **Web (JS)** |
| **Zoom Video SDK** — custom video apps (not "join a Zoom meeting") | same set as Meeting SDK |
| **Zoom Meeting Bot SDK (Linux)** — headless bot that joins as participant | C++ only |
| **Zoom REST API + Webhooks** | language-agnostic (HTTP) |

No official or community Go bindings exist for any client/bot SDK. People who want a Zoom meeting bot without writing C++ typically pay [Recall.ai](https://recall.ai) to wrap it.

## Mapped to the three deployment shapes (from antiphon.md §Mic control)

### (a) AI is the host — bot joins as participant
- Captures audio of all participants, can mute them as host.
- Requires Meeting Bot SDK (C++) **or** Recall.ai (service, $$/minute).
- **Go is not viable for the bot itself.** Could be Go orchestrator + C++ bot, or Go orchestrator + Recall.ai.

### (b) Build outside Zoom (v2)
- Our own WebRTC stack. Backend in anything. UI is browser.
- **Go is fine for backend.** UI is JS regardless.

### (c) Audio relay in front of Zoom (v1 wedge)
- User stays on Zoom. Their mic is routed through our process via PipeWire/PulseAudio virtual sinks.
- We control the gain on their outbound audio: pass / duck / mute, on command from the allocator.
- **Pure OS audio plumbing — Go is correct here.** No Zoom SDK touched.

## Decision

- **Go** for v1 audio relay (`cmd/antiphon-relay`), allocator/policy daemon (`cmd/antiphon-mod`), any future REST/webhook services.
- **TypeScript/React** for UI prototypes and any eventual Zoom Apps SDK panel (forced by Zoom).
- **C++ or Recall.ai** is a v2+ decision only, gated on whether option (a) becomes the target deployment. Defer.

## Consequences

- v1 ships without any Zoom SDK dependency. Lowest-friction path.
- If we ever want a participant-bot deployment (option a), we accept a polyglot codebase or a Recall.ai dependency. Neither is in scope now.
- The Zoom Apps SDK panel (showing the bid heat-map inside Zoom) is a v1.5/v2 question. The standalone web prototype in `prototypes/bid-heatmap-ui/` is the design rehearsal.

# ADR 0002 — Platform-agnostic core with pluggable adapters

**Date**: 2026-05-20
**Status**: accepted
**Supersedes part of**: antiphon.md §Mic control framing that puts Zoom at the center

## Question

User pivot: "if we don't have to be tied to zoom that's great, but we should make it easy to integrate with zoom and meet."

Original design treated **option (c) audio relay in front of Zoom** as THE v1 wedge. That couples the product to Zoom and bakes a workaround into the architecture.

## Decision

Build the **core platform-agnostic**. Treat Zoom and Google Meet as adapters, not as load-bearing dependencies.

```
                ┌──────────────────────────────────┐
                │   antiphon core (Go)             │
                │                                  │
                │   allocator · bid channel ·      │
                │   policy · HUD logic · audit log │
                └──────────────────────────────────┘
                              ▲
                              │  PlatformAdapter interface
                              │  (capture audio, control mic,
                              │   join/leave, list participants)
                              │
        ┌─────────────┬───────┴───────┬──────────────┐
        │             │               │              │
   ┌────▼────┐   ┌────▼────┐    ┌─────▼────┐   ┌─────▼─────┐
   │ native  │   │ zoom-   │    │ meet-    │   │ bot-via-  │
   │ WebRTC  │   │ relay   │    │ relay    │   │ recall.ai │
   │ (own UI)│   │ (PCM)   │    │ (PCM)    │   │ (Zoom/    │
   │         │   │         │    │          │   │  Meet)    │
   └─────────┘   └─────────┘    └──────────┘   └───────────┘
```

## Adapter interface (sketch)

```go
type PlatformAdapter interface {
    // identify participants — opaque IDs scoped to a meeting session
    Participants(ctx context.Context) ([]ParticipantID, error)

    // audio capture: per-participant if the platform allows it,
    // else a single mixed stream w/ best-effort diarization upstream
    AudioFrames(ctx context.Context) (<-chan AudioFrame, error)

    // mic control — the platform's enforcement layer
    SetGain(ctx context.Context, id ParticipantID, gain float32) error // [0,1]

    // private feedback channel back to a single participant
    // (HUD overlays, countdown to yield, etc.)
    SendPrivateHUD(ctx context.Context, id ParticipantID, msg HUDEvent) error

    Capabilities() Capabilities  // what this adapter actually supports
}

type Capabilities struct {
    PerParticipantAudio bool  // false for zoom-relay (only local user's mic)
    GainControl         bool  // true for relay + native, false for view-only adapters
    PrivateHUD          bool
    VideoIn             bool  // for the multimodal arbitration substrate
}
```

The capability flags let the allocator gracefully degrade: a `zoom-relay` adapter only has gain control over the local user's outbound mic, not other participants — the allocator still runs but its enforcement surface is smaller.

## v1 implementation order

1. **Audio relay primitive** (Go binary, PCM-in/PCM-out + UDS control). Substrate for both `zoom-relay` and `meet-relay` — neither cares which app is generating the audio on the OS. → `prototypes/audio-relay-spike/`, then promoted to `internal/relay/`.
2. **Bid heat-map UI** (React/TS prototype). Platform-independent. → `prototypes/bid-heatmap-ui/`.
3. **Allocator** consuming bid stream + producing gain commands against the `PlatformAdapter` interface.
4. **First real adapter**: probably native WebRTC (full vision demo) OR `zoom-relay` (lowest friction for early users). TBD.

## Consequences

- Zoom-specific code lives in `internal/adapter/zoomrelay/` and never leaks upward.
- Adding Google Meet is "implement the interface" — Meet's Add-ons SDK is JS-only for in-meeting panels, but the relay path doesn't need it (audio is OS-level).
- Bot-as-host (option a) becomes `internal/adapter/recall/` — a service-backed adapter — deferred.
- The "v1 wedge is audio relay in front of Zoom" framing in antiphon.md is downgraded: it's _one_ adapter, not the architecture.

1. **Audio relay primitive** (Go binary, PCM-in/PCM-out + UDS control). Substrate for both `zoom-relay` and `meet-relay` — neither cares which app is generating the audio on the OS. → `prototypes/audio-relay-spike/`, then promoted to `internal/relay/`.
2. **Bid heat-map UI** (React/TS prototype). Platform-independent. → `prototypes/bid-heatmap-ui/`.
3. **Allocator** consuming bid stream + producing gain commands against the `PlatformAdapter` interface.
4. **First real adapter**: probably native WebRTC (full vision demo) OR `zoom-relay` (lowest friction for early users). TBD.

## Consequences

- Zoom-specific code lives in `internal/adapter/zoomrelay/` and never leaks upward.
- Adding Google Meet is "implement the interface" — Meet's Add-ons SDK is JS-only for in-meeting panels, but the relay path doesn't need it (audio is OS-level).
- Bot-as-host (option a) becomes `internal/adapter/recall/` — a service-backed adapter — deferred.
- The "v1 wedge is audio relay in front of Zoom" framing in antiphon.md is downgraded: it's _one_ adapter, not the architecture.

# audio-relay spike

Proves: a Go process can sit in the audio path and apply a controllable
gain (mute / duck / pass) with low latency, controlled via a Unix socket.

This is the substrate for the `zoomrelay` and `meetrelay` adapters
(ADR 0002) — neither of which need a vendor SDK; both just need OS audio
plumbing routed through this primitive.

## What's already built

The primitive lives in `internal/relay/`. The CLI wrapper is
`cmd/antiphon-relay`. This directory has only the demo scripts.

```
cd ../..
go build -o ./bin/antiphon-relay ./cmd/antiphon-relay
```

## Demo 1 — standalone sine wave (no Zoom, no PipeWire surgery)

Verifies the gain primitive end-to-end without touching the OS audio stack.

```
./demo.sh                  # one terminal: 440 Hz tone through the relay
./mute.sh                  # another terminal: tone goes silent
./unmute.sh                # tone returns
./duck.sh                  # tone drops to 0.3 gain
```

Needs `ffmpeg` (for the sine source and playback) and `socat` or `nc -U`
(for the control socket).

## Demo 2 — in front of Zoom or Meet (PipeWire/PulseAudio wiring)

The production wiring. Creates a virtual mic that Zoom/Meet select as
their input, with our relay binary in the middle of the audio path.

See `../../scripts/wire-relay.sh` — a one-shot setup script. After
running it:

1. In Zoom/Meet, pick input device "Antiphon Mic".
2. Speak normally. Your real mic is routed through the relay.
3. `echo MUTE | socat - UNIX-CONNECT:/tmp/antiphon-relay.sock` — Zoom
   hears silence even though you keep talking. No Zoom SDK touched.

The key property: **the user's own Zoom client still believes they are
unmuted.** The allocator gains the audio at the OS layer, below Zoom's
mute UI. This is what makes it a wedge — Zoom can't tell.

## Control protocol

Line-oriented, one command per line, reply per command.

```
MUTE          -> OK 0
UNMUTE        -> OK 1
DUCK          -> OK 0.3
GAIN 0.65     -> OK 0.65
GET           -> OK <current>
```

## Latency

Chunk size is 4096 bytes = 2048 samples at s16le. At 48 kHz that is ~42 ms
per chunk — well inside the 150–200 ms allocation budget from antiphon.md
§Operational requirements. Drop the chunk to 1024 bytes (~10 ms) if a
real-world measurement shows the larger chunk matters.

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Standalone demo: 440 Hz tone → relay → speakers. No PulseAudio surgery.
# Mute/unmute/duck via ./mute.sh, ./unmute.sh, ./duck.sh in another terminal.
set -euo pipefail

ROOT="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")/../.." && pwd)"
RELAY="${ROOT}/bin/antiphon-relay"
SOCK="/tmp/antiphon-relay.sock"

if [[ ! -x "${RELAY}" ]]; then
  echo "building ${RELAY} ..." >&2
  (cd "${ROOT}" && go build -o "${RELAY}" ./cmd/antiphon-relay)
fi

if ! command -v ffmpeg >/dev/null; then
  echo "this demo needs ffmpeg (sudo apt install ffmpeg)" >&2
  exit 1
fi

echo "Tone playing. In another terminal: ./mute.sh / ./unmute.sh / ./duck.sh"
echo "Ctrl-C to stop."

ffmpeg -hide_banner -loglevel error \
  -f lavfi -i 'sine=frequency=440:duration=120' \
  -f s16le -ar 48000 -ac 1 - \
| "${RELAY}" -ctl "${SOCK}" -gain 1.0 \
| ffplay -hide_banner -loglevel error \
  -f s16le -ar 48000 -ac 1 -nodisp -autoexit -

#!/usr/bin/env bash
# wire-relay.sh — set up a virtual "Antiphon Mic" device that pipes the
# user's real mic through our relay binary before Zoom/Meet sees it.
#
# Works on PulseAudio (Ubuntu 22.04 and earlier) and on PipeWire's
# Pulse compatibility layer (Ubuntu 22.10+, Fedora 35+, etc.) — both
# accept `pactl`.
#
# Tear down with: ./wire-relay.sh down
set -euo pipefail

ROOT="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")/.." && pwd)"
RELAY="${ROOT}/bin/antiphon-relay"
SOCK="/tmp/antiphon-relay.sock"
SINK_NAME="antiphon_tap"     # internal: user's mic flows in here
SOURCE_NAME="antiphon_mic"   # what Zoom selects as input
STATE_FILE="/tmp/antiphon-wire-state"

case "${1:-up}" in
  up)
    if [[ ! -x "${RELAY}" ]]; then
      echo "building ${RELAY} ..." >&2
      (cd "${ROOT}" && go build -o "${RELAY}" ./cmd/antiphon-relay)
    fi

    # 1) virtual sink to capture the user's real mic
    M1=$(pactl load-module module-null-sink \
      sink_name="${SINK_NAME}" \
      sink_properties=device.description="Antiphon-Tap")

    # 2) virtual source that Zoom/Meet will pick as input
    M2=$(pactl load-module module-null-sink \
      sink_name="${SOURCE_NAME}_sink" \
      sink_properties=device.description="Antiphon-Mic-Sink")
    M3=$(pactl load-module module-remap-source \
      source_name="${SOURCE_NAME}" \
      master="${SOURCE_NAME}_sink.monitor" \
      source_properties=device.description="Antiphon-Mic")

    # 3) loopback the user's real mic INTO the tap sink
    DEFAULT_SOURCE=$(pactl info | awk -F': ' '/Default Source/{print $2}')
    M4=$(pactl load-module module-loopback \
      source="${DEFAULT_SOURCE}" \
      sink="${SINK_NAME}" \
      latency_msec=20)

    # 4) run the relay: stdin = monitor of the tap, stdout = the mic sink
    echo "${M1} ${M2} ${M3} ${M4}" > "${STATE_FILE}"
    echo "antiphon: virtual devices loaded. Modules: $(cat "${STATE_FILE}")"
    echo "antiphon: starting relay; pick 'Antiphon-Mic' in Zoom/Meet input."

    exec parec --device="${SINK_NAME}.monitor" \
      --format=s16le --rate=48000 --channels=1 \
    | "${RELAY}" -ctl "${SOCK}" -gain 1.0 \
    | pacat --device="${SOURCE_NAME}_sink" \
      --format=s16le --rate=48000 --channels=1 --latency-msec=20
    ;;

  down)
    if [[ -f "${STATE_FILE}" ]]; then
      for mod in $(cat "${STATE_FILE}"); do
        pactl unload-module "${mod}" || true
      done
      rm -f "${STATE_FILE}"
      echo "antiphon: virtual devices unloaded"
    else
      echo "antiphon: no state file at ${STATE_FILE}; nothing to do"
    fi
    ;;

  *)
    echo "usage: $0 [up|down]" >&2
    exit 2
    ;;
esac

# bid-heatmap UI spike

Feel-prototype for the continuous bid + reason-tagged + AI-allocated UI
described in `docs/antiphon.md §Embrace`.

## Run

```
npm install
npm run dev
# open http://localhost:5173
```

## What it demonstrates

- **Continuous bid intensity** per participant (slider 0..1) rendered as a
  public heat-map bar — not a discrete raise-hand.
- **Reason-tagged bids** — each bid carries `question / disagree / build-on /
  time-check`. The reason colors the bar and gates the allocator weighting.
- **Speaker-aware UI** — current speaker is bordered + scaled up, on-deck
  is highlighted, idle participants fade. Single visual surface; everyone
  sees the same thing.
- **Mode-switching policy** — `brainstorm / decide / inform / socialize`
  load different reason-weight tables (see `src/allocator.ts`).
- **Soft turn cap → handoff pressure** — the longer the current speaker
  holds, the lower the bid threshold to take the floor. Visible as a %
  in the header.
- **Quiet-bidder boost** — DiMicco 2004 caveat fix: low-airtime participants
  get a multiplicative score bump so visibility-without-coercion doesn't
  default to silence.
- **`why` line per participant** — the allocator's score breakdown, exposed.
  Published policy is the procedural-justice answer from `docs/antiphon.md`
  §Operational requirements.

## What it does NOT demonstrate yet

- No real audio. Bids are sliders; speaker is whoever the allocator picks.
- No video / HUD overlays. The private speaker-only HUD lives elsewhere.
- No multimodal arbitration. The real allocator pulls signal from audio
  prosody + gaze, not just sliders.
- No latency simulation. Real version has the ~150–200 ms allocation
  budget from Stivers 2009; this runs at 5 Hz for legibility.

# ADR 0003 — Distribution model: companion app + bot adapter

**Date**: 2026-05-20
**Status**: accepted
**Revises**: ADR 0002's framing of "audio relay = v1"

## Question

User: "ok but not every meeting attendee will have a similar setup to mine."

The audio-relay primitive (`prototypes/audio-relay-spike/`) only sits in front of **one participant's** OS audio stack — the one running the relay binary with PipeWire/PulseAudio surgery. It cannot:

- See or influence the other 4–11 people in the call.
- Show the bid heat-map to anyone but the local user.
- Enforce floor allocation across the meeting.
- Work on macOS or Windows attendees without a parallel install + OS-specific virtual-device shims (BlackHole, VB-Cable).
- Work on mobile attendees at all (no audio pipeline access).

So the relay is **not a distribution model**. It's a useful primitive in a narrow niche. The product needs a way to reach _every_ participant in a meeting without asking each of them to do anything technical.

## Distribution shapes (realistic, ranked by spread × friction)

### 1. Companion browser app — primary v1 path

`antiphon.app/m/<meeting-id>` opens in a browser tab alongside whatever video tool the meeting is on (Zoom, Meet, Teams, in-person-with-laptops, etc.). Every participant opens the link.

What it provides:
- The public reason-tagged bid heat-map (each participant's continuous bid + reason tag, visible to everyone).
- The on-deck indicator, speaker-aware UI.
- Private HUD overlays in-tab for the current speaker.
- Visible time budgets, mode declaration, handoff pressure.
- The allocator's published policy + per-decision diagnostic.

What it lacks:
- **No hard mic control.** It can _ask_ a participant to yield ("Sara, wrap in 10s — Devraj has a strong disagree bid"); it can't take their mic.
- This is the DiMicco 2004 path: visibility + social cost as enforcement. The asymmetry stings (works on loud speakers, doesn't reliably raise quiet ones) — the quiet-bidder boost in `internal/allocator` is the design response.

Why this is the right v1:
- Zero install for any participant on any OS.
- Decouples us from Zoom/Meet — same companion works alongside any video tool, or even an in-person meeting with laptops.
- The visibility surface is the most novel part of the design (continuous, reason-tagged, AI-allocated heat-map). Ships independently of mic control.

### 2. Bot adapter (Recall.ai or Zoom Meeting Bot SDK) — v1.5 hard-enforcement layer

A bot joins the Zoom/Meet/Teams call as a participant. As a co-host, it has mute authority over others (Zoom; Meet is more limited). Antiphon's allocator commands the bot to mute/unmute.

- Recall.ai abstracts Zoom/Meet/Teams/Webex behind one API; charges per minute.
- Zoom Meeting Bot SDK (C++ on Linux) is the build-it-ourselves path; more work, no per-minute cost.
- Either way, **the participants need to do nothing** — the bot is on the host's side.

Combined with (1), this gives you: companion app visible to everyone + hard mic control via the bot, with no per-participant install.

### 3. Audio relay (the old "v1 wedge") — power-user only

Useful for:
- The host or a technical participant who wants local self-discipline tooling (the relay can refuse to unmute past their budget, force-duck on a moderator command, etc.).
- A development-time substrate for testing allocator decisions against real audio without a meeting bot in the loop.
- A future fallback for participants on platforms we don't have a bot adapter for yet.

**Not the primary distribution.** Don't pitch it as such.

### 4. Native WebRTC (Antiphon-as-platform) — v2

Build our own video tool. Full control, full vision. Asks users to leave Zoom — high lift, only justifiable once the companion app has demonstrated demand.

## Decision

- Build the **companion browser app** as the primary v1 distribution.
- Use the existing `prototypes/bid-heatmap-ui` as the seed; promote to a real package (`apps/companion/`) once the shape settles.
- Wire **Recall.ai** as the first bot adapter for hard enforcement (v1.5). Companion app participants get visibility immediately; the host opts into the bot when they want mic enforcement.
- Keep the audio relay as a niche tool, not the architecture's center of gravity.

## Adapter interface implications

The `PlatformAdapter` from ADR 0002 still holds, but the interesting adapters become:

```
internal/adapter/companion/   — drives the browser-app side (WebSocket fanout)
internal/adapter/recall/      — bot-as-host via Recall.ai (mic enforcement)
internal/adapter/zoomrelay/   — niche, power-user
internal/adapter/meetrelay/   — niche, power-user
internal/adapter/native/      — v2
```

The companion adapter has `GainScope = GainScopeNone` — it can only request, not enforce. The recall adapter has `GainScopeAll`. The relay adapters have `GainScopeLocalOnly`.

## Consequences

- The bid-heatmap-ui spike is now load-bearing — it _is_ the v1 distribution surface in miniature. Treat it as one.
- We commit to a backend (the allocator + WebSocket fanout for the companion). Go, per ADR 0001.
- We accept a Recall.ai dependency for v1.5 enforcement, or budget the C++ Meeting Bot work.
- Audio relay code stays in tree as a primitive; its README scope is downgraded.

# Security policy

## Reporting a vulnerability

Please report security issues privately via GitHub's
[private vulnerability reporting](https://github.com/justinstimatze/antiphon/security/advisories/new),
not as a public issue.

We aim to acknowledge reports within 72 hours and to ship fixes or a
disclosure timeline within 14 days for confirmed issues.

## Scope

This repository hosts an in-development design + prototype codebase for
an AI meeting moderator. No production deployments yet. Reports should
focus on:

- Server-side vulnerabilities in the (future) companion app backend
- Audio-relay primitive misuse (it sits in the system audio path)
- Allocator policy that could be gamed adversarially in a way the
  visibility surface can't surface
- Secrets / credentials accidentally committed

## Out of scope

- Findings from automated scanners with no proof of exploitability
- Social-engineering scenarios outside the product surface
- Issues in third-party services we integrate with (report to them directly)

args.content:
# v2.2 — ground the critic on real recruitment-ancestor prose

## Context

The v2.1 critic (`cmd/cupel/critic.go`) diagnoses each engine prototype's **separation
margin** on a held-out corpus and proposes ablation-gated prototype edits. Today that corpus
is the **synthetic** `builtinProbeCorpus` in `probe.go` — ~2 hand-written recruitment
sentences per engine. HANDOFF priority #1 (v2.2): the synthetic corpus can overfit the
prototypes to its own register, so point `CUPEL_CRITIC_CORPUS` at the **real
recruitment-ancestor prose** the v1-recall failure was actually measured on
(Robison/Kybalion/Trine/Ovid/Sumner/Pericles — the texts in `/tmp/pg*.txt`).

The critic already supports the override: `probe.go:82` loads `CUPEL_CRITIC_CORPUS` as a JSON
array of `{"engine","text"}`. What's missing is a **reproducible way to produce that JSON** from
the ancestor texts. The extraction logic already exists — `gutenbergParagraphs`, `strideSample`,
and the `ancestorSources` engine-mapping — but it's trapped inside `corpus_recall_test.go` (a
`_test.go` file, not reusable). The texts themselves are ephemeral (`/tmp`, re-downloaded each
session per HANDOFF), so a one-shot hand-built JSON would rot. The right artifact is a small
`cupel build-corpus` subcommand that regenerates the corpus on demand.

Intended outcome: a committed, reproducible builder; a critic run grounded in real prose; a
recorded comparison of real-vs-synthetic margins for the overlapping engines (the overfitting
signal v2.2 exists to expose). The **second half** of v2.2 — re-tuning `CUPEL_GATE_THRESHOLD`
from `doctor`'s `gate_sim` distribution — stays **blocked** (zero live v2 fires; nothing to tune
from) and is explicitly out of scope this pass.

## Approach

### 1. NEW `cmd/cupel/ancestors.go` — promote the extraction + add the builder
Move three declarations **out of** `corpus_recall_test.go` into this non-test file (same package,
so the test keeps compiling unchanged):
- `ancestorSources` (the Gutenberg-id → engine dual-use mapping),
- `gutenbergParagraphs(raw string, minLen int) []string`,
- `strideSample(xs []string, max int) []string`.

Add:
- `buildAncestorCorpus(dir string, maxPerEngine int, fillSynthetic bool) ([]probeExample, []string, error)`
  — for each `ancestorSources` entry whose file exists in `dir`: read, `gutenbergParagraphs(raw, 200)`,
  `strideSample(_, maxPerEngine)`, emit one `probeExample{Engine, Text}` per paragraph. Append the
  benign **negatives** from `builtinProbeCorpus` (Engine==""). If `fillSynthetic`, also append the
  builtin POS for any engine with **no** ancestor file (full-coverage option). Return the corpus plus
  a per-engine count summary. Pure text — needs neither ollama nor a key.
- `runBuildCorpus(args []string)` — flags `--dir` (default `$CUPEL_ANCESTOR_DIR` or `/tmp`),
  `--out` (default stdout; a path writes the file), `--max N` (per-engine cap, default 50),
  `--fill-synthetic` (default off). Calls `buildAncestorCorpus`, `json.MarshalIndent`, writes,
  prints the per-engine count summary (clearly marking which engines are real-grounded vs absent).

**Default is real-only** (no `--fill-synthetic`): real ancestor POS for the engines with texts +
the realistic modern-benign NEG. This is the cleanest calibration bed (uniform real recruitment
register vs the modern-benign prompts the live gate actually sees) and keeps the real-vs-synthetic
margin comparison clean. Engines without an ancestor text are simply absent — the critic's
`diagnoseEngines` leaves them at the `OwnFloor=2` sentinel and never flags them (`critic.go:116`),
a legible "no real grounding yet" gap, not a crash.

### 2. EDIT `cmd/cupel/corpus_recall_test.go` — remove the moved decls
Delete the three declarations now living in `ancestors.go` (lines ~33-44 `ancestorSources`,
~49-59 `strideSample`, ~65-83 `gutenbergParagraphs`). Keep `embedInChunks`,
`TestRealCorpusGateRecall`, and the local `srcStat` type. No import or behavior change.

### 3. EDIT `cmd/cupel/main.go` — dispatch + help
- `case "build-corpus": runBuildCorpus(os.Args[2:])` in the `switch` (`main.go:73`).
- A `build-corpus` usage block in the `help` const, documenting it as the v2.2 corpus generator
  and noting the output feeds `CUPEL_CRITIC_CORPUS`.

### 4. Regenerate, run, compare (the actual v2.2 act)
- `go build ./cmd/cupel && gofmt -l cmd/cupel` (clean) and `CUPEL_ENV_FILE=$PWD/.env go test ./cmd/cupel`
  (green; recall test still skips unless `CUPEL_ANCESTOR_DIR` is set).
- `cupel build-corpus --dir /tmp --out /tmp/cupel-critic-corpus.json` → inspect the per-engine summary.
- Baseline: `CUPEL_ENV_FILE=$PWD/.env cupel critic --max 6` (synthetic default) — capture the margin table.
- Grounded: `CUPEL_ENV_FILE=$PWD/.env CUPEL_CRITIC_CORPUS=/tmp/cupel-critic-corpus.json cupel critic --max 6`
  — capture the margin table + proposals. `--max 6` so every real-grounded engine is diagnosed
  (Haiku calls are cached by content hash; a re-run is free).
- `git status` → **confirm `cmd/cupel/engines.json` is unchanged** (critic is propose-only).
- Record the real-vs-synthetic margin shift for the overlapping engines: a large shift = the
  synthetic corpus was overfitting (the falsifiable finding v2.2 was built to surface).

### 5. EDIT docs — `cmd/cupel/README.md`, `HANDOFF.md`, `entries/_backlog.md`
- README: document `build-corpus` and the v2.2 grounding workflow (regen → `CUPEL_CRITIC_CORPUS` → critic).
- HANDOFF: mark v2.2 part-1 (real-corpus grounding) **done**, record the real-vs-synthetic finding,
  and keep part-2 (gate-threshold re-tune) flagged **blocked on zero live fires**.
- `_backlog.md`: move the v2.2 line from queued to built; note the corpus JSON is derived/ephemeral
  (regenerate via `build-corpus`, **not committed**).

### Deliberately NOT done
- **`engines.json` untouched** — the critic only proposes; the substrate stays human-gated.
- **`CUPEL_GATE_THRESHOLD` untouched** — re-tuning needs `doctor`'s `gate_sim` distribution, which
  needs live v2 fires (zero so far). Blocked; named for later.
- **The corpus JSON is not committed** — it's derived from ephemeral `/tmp` public-domain texts and
  is large; the *builder* is the committed reproducibility artifact (mirrors the texts being local-only).
- `critic.go`, `probe.go`, `gate.go`, `client.go`, `hook.go` — unchanged (the builder is additive).

## Files
- **NEW** `cmd/cupel/ancestors.go` — moved extraction decls + `buildAncestorCorpus` + `runBuildCorpus`.
- **EDIT** `cmd/cupel/corpus_recall_test.go` — remove the three moved declarations.
- **EDIT** `cmd/cupel/main.go` — `build-corpus` dispatch + help block.
- **EDIT** `cmd/cupel/README.md`, `HANDOFF.md`, `entries/_backlog.md` — document v2.2.
- **READ-only reuse**: `probe.go` (`probeExample`, `builtinProbeCorpus`, the `CUPEL_CRITIC_CORPUS`
  loader), `critic.go` (consumer; unchanged), `calib.go`/`hook.go` helpers (`envInt`).

## Verification
1. `go build ./cmd/cupel` clean; `gofmt -l cmd/cupel` empty.
2. `CUPEL_ENV_FILE=$PWD/.env go test ./cmd/cupel` green (recall test still skips without
   `CUPEL_ANCESTOR_DIR`; moving its helpers didn't change behavior).
3. `cupel build-corpus --dir /tmp --out /tmp/cupel-critic-corpus.json` writes valid JSON; the
   per-engine summary shows real counts for the 6 ancestor engines + the benign NEG.
4. The grounded `cupel critic --max 6` run prints a real-prose margin table and writes proposals to
   `~/.claude/cupel/critic-proposals/`; `git status` confirms `engines.json` unchanged.
5. A second grounded run re-uses the Haiku proposal cache (no new API spend; local re-embed only).
6. Commit via `git commit -F /tmp/msg.txt` (avoid the word "which"; `Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7
   (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>`). Stage explicitly — never `git add -A` (the gitignored
   `.env` must never be staged); do not stage the `/tmp` corpus JSON.
